planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; mountain death camas requires good winter cold and is best suited to northern regions and mountain elevations.

directions: Anticlea elegans (formerly Zigadenus elegans, white camas) is a graceful bulbous perennial native to moist mountain meadows, subalpine slopes, and streambanks across western and central North America from Alaska to New Mexico, producing a basal cluster of narrow, grass-like leaves and an erect stem in summer bearing a loose raceme of saucer-shaped, creamy white to greenish-white flowers with distinctive greenish glands at the petal bases. Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; the plant requires good winter cold and is best suited to northern regions and mountain elevations. All parts of the plant are highly toxic — it is closely related to and easily confused with edible camas (Camassia), so never harvest from mixed populations without expert identification. An elegant addition to a native wildflower garden in cool climates.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Virginia bunchflower requires consistently moist conditions and does best in wet meadows or along stream margins.

directions: Melanthium virginicum is a stately native perennial of eastern North American wet meadows, bogs, and stream margins, producing tall stems 3–5 feet in height from a clump of long, strap-like leaves, topped in summer (June–July) with a large, showy, pyramidal panicle of creamy white to pale greenish-white flowers that age to deep purple-green, creating a striking color transition over several weeks of bloom. It grows naturally in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soils in partial shade to full sun and is an excellent choice for rain garden margins, pond banks, and moist native meadow plantings in the mid-Atlantic and Southeast. The large flower panicles provide nectar for native bees and insects; leaves have a pleated, corn-like texture. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested; handle rhizomes with gloves.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial shade; fairy wand is dioecious — plant in groups of 3–5 to ensure both male and female plants are present for seed production.

directions: Chamaelirium luteum is an elegant and architecturally distinct native perennial of eastern North American deciduous forests and woodland margins, producing a basal rosette of smooth, spatula-shaped, deep-green leaves from which arise wand-like flowering stems in spring — male plants bearing slender, arching, horizontal spikes of tiny white flowers, female plants bearing stouter, upright white spikes that eventually bear seed capsules — giving the two sexes a strikingly different appearance. It grows in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to light shade from New England to Florida, and the species name luteum (yellow) refers to the yellow-green coloration of the rhizome, called "devil's bit" by herbalists because the tip appears bitten off. Plant in groups of 3–5 to ensure male and female plants are present for pollination and seed set. The plant is slow to establish but extremely long-lived and very deer-resistant.

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated divisions in fall in wet, peaty, acidic soil in partial shade; swamp pink is federally threatened — purchase only nursery-propagated stock and never collect from the wild.

directions: Helonias bullata (swamp pink) is a federally threatened native perennial found in a handful of isolated, high-quality, seepage-fed bogs and wet woods in the mid-Atlantic and upper Southeast, where it produces an overwintering rosette of lustrous, strap-like, evergreen leaves and a dramatic, hollow stem bearing a dense, cylindrical spike of fragrant, bright rosy-pink flowers with blue anthers in March and April — one of the earliest and most vivid wildflowers of the eastern bog flora. It requires permanently wet, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), peaty, oxygen-poor soil in partial shade with steady seepage from upslope, making it a challenging but rewarding plant for constructed bog gardens or reliably wet rain garden margins. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants from specialists — the species is protected and increasingly rare due to wetland drainage and deer browsing. An extraordinary native gem for conservation-minded gardeners.

planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery plants in spring in wet, acidic, peaty or sandy bog soil in full sun to partial shade; bog asphodel requires year-round wet conditions and strongly acidic soil (pH 3.5–5.0).

directions: Narthecium americanum is a rare and beautiful native bog perennial of the Atlantic Coastal Plain, found only in a narrow strip of New Jersey, Delaware, and adjacent states in the open, wet, acidic, sphagnum-moss bog and pine barren habitats of the mid-Atlantic region, where it produces a fan-like tuft of narrow, iris-like leaves and a terminal spike of bright yellow star-shaped flowers with woolly yellow stamens in June and July, followed by ornamental orange-red seed capsules in fall. It requires year-round wet, strongly acidic (pH 3.5–5.0) conditions and is extremely sensitive to soil chemistry — it will not survive in alkaline or enriched soils. Grow in a constructed Sphagnum moss bog or wet sand medium with no added fertilizers; the species is a flagship plant for the globally rare Atlantic Coastal Plain bog community. Available only from specialist native plant nurseries.
sources: New Jersey Pine Barrens Native Plant Society
